courses.subject: CLCE
courses.title: Chinese Language, Culture and Language Learning

meetings.location: Lee Shau Kee Archi Bldg G03

courses.description:
(UGED1146 is double-coded with CLCE1103.)

This is a knowledge-based course introducing Chinese language and culture. The course is designed for CUHK students (both native and non-native Chinese) who are interested in Chinese language and culture; Contents included theories and applications concerning Chinese language and culture; theories, practices and situations of teaching and learning Chinese as a second language (including Putonghua and Cantonese learning); the importance of culture in Chinese as a second language; and China’s language policy.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
CLCE1103 is double-coded with UGED1146. 
Not for students who have taken CCSS1010, UGED1146 and UGED2760.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students are expected to:

1. acquire some basic knowledge about Chinese language varieties and recognize their relationship to Chinese culture.

2. be able to develop critical mind in evaluating and appreciating Chinese language and its associated cultures.

3. be able to understand major discourses about language situations and related cultural issues in Hong Kong SAR and in Chinese Mainland.

4. be able to discuss some foundation theories and practices in teaching and learning Chinese as a second language.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *(A) Major Topics**

1. Understanding Chinese language and culture (introduce the relationship between language and culture with specific reference to Chinese language varieties)

2. Language in communication: the learning process (introduce key concepts and development of teaching and learning Chinese as a second/foreign language [CSL]; discuss basic principles in CSL)

3. Standard Chinese and varieties: a diachronic approach (discuss China’s language policy; development of Mandarin Chinese; development of pinyin system and Chinese scripts)

4. Standard Chinese and varieties: a synchronic approach (discuss Chinese varieties used in different regions in China as well as in different parts of the world)

5. Languages and cultures in Hong Kong SAR (discuss language policy of HKSAR; status of different languages, and sociolinguistic issues)

6. Cantonese and other major dialects in China (discuss Cantonese and its relationship with other major dialects in China)

7. Learning Chinese: listening and speaking (introduce basic principles in teaching and learning Chinese focusing on teaching and learning listening and speaking skills with reference to linguistics, pragmatic and cultural aspects)

8. Learning Chinese: reading and writing (introduce basic principles in teaching and learning Chinese focusing on teaching and learning reading and writing skills with reference to linguistics, pragmatic and cultural aspects)

9. Appreciating language through culture (discuss Chinese varieties appearing in culture artifacts)

10. Appreciating culture through language (discuss culturally loaded idioms, proverbs, and stories; and drawn relationship between Chinese culture and language)

11. Wrap up and review of major concepts
courses.feedback_evaluation:
1. Course and Teaching Evaluation Survey
2. Internal Course Review
Others:
courses.required_readings:
Required Readings on the relationship between language and culture

1. Greetings, conversations and farewells. In Encountering the Chinese. W. Hu and C. Grove. 1991. Yarmouth, Me.: Intercultural Press. P.21-33.

2. Pragmatic framework and its role in language learning: With special reference to Chinese. In Processes and Process-Orientation in Foreign Language Teaching and Learning. W. Chan et al (eds.). 2011. De Gruter Mouton. P.65-89.

3. The relationship of language and culture. In Language and Culture. C. Kramsch. 1998. Oxford: Oxford University Press. P.3-14.

Required Readings on the language policy and sociolinguistic issues in China

4. The modern standard language I. In Chinese. J Norman. 1988. Cambridge University Press. P.133-151.

5. Dialectal variation in North and Central China. In Chinese. J Norman. 1988. Cambridge University Press. P.181-209.

6. The use of English and Chinese Languages in Hong Kong. In Language Learning & Communication. Cheung, Yat-shing. 1984. 3(3) P.273-283*.*

7. One Country, Two Cultures and Three Languages: Sociolinguistic Conditions and Language Education in Hong Kong. In Teaching Language and Culture: Building Hong Kong on Education. B. Asker (ed.). 1998. Hong Kong: Addison Wesley Longman China. P.152-175.

Required Readings on development and trends in teaching and learning Chinese as a second language

8. Task, listening comprehension and SLA. In Task-based Language Learning and Teaching. R. Ellis. 2009. Oxford University Press. P.37-68.

9. On the Chinese Characters. In The Creative Alphabets in the Whole World. C.Y. SIN. 2009. Korea. San Chung Pub. Co. P.1-11.

Students are expected to read all chapters listed under "Required Readings", which are available in pdf format on course online platform for those who are registered.
courses.recommended_readings:
1. Norman, J. (1988). Chinese. Cambridge University Press.

2. Kramsch, C. (1998). Language and Culture. Oxford University Press.

Students are encouraged to read other relevant chapters from books under "Required readings", as well those listed under "Recommended Reading".
